{"success": true, "name": "self-hosted-meeting-infrastructure", "description": "Deploy and operate self-hosted meeting transcription infrastructure — Vexa meeting bots, Speaches (Whisper ASR), MinIO storage, and the delivery pipeline for recordings + transcripts + summaries. Covers Docker networking, Traefik integration alongside existing Swarm services, and Telegram notification wiring.", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: self-hosted-meeting-infrastructure
description: Deploy and operate self-hosted meeting transcription infrastructure — Vexa meeting bots, Speaches (Whisper ASR), MinIO storage, and the delivery pipeline for recordings + transcripts + summaries. Covers Docker networking, Traefik integration alongside existing Swarm services, and Telegram notification wiring.
version: 1.0.0
author: erebus
license: Apache 2.0
---

# Self-Hosted Meeting Infrastructure

Class-level skill for deploying meeting bots that join Google Meet/Teams/Zoom, record audio, transcribe locally via CPU Whisper, and deliver results via Telegram. Runs alongside existing Docker Swarm services on a single VPS.

## Architecture

```
Browser → meet.paragu-ai.com (dashboard)
         → api.paragu-ai.com (API gateway)

Vexa stack (docker compose)          Speaches (standalone)
┌─────────────────────────┐          ┌──────────────────┐
│ Dashboard :3000          │          │ Whisper API :8000│
│ API Gateway :8056        │          │ (CPU, int8)      │
│ Meeting API :8080        │──audio──▶│                  │
│ MinIO :9002 (recordings) │          └──────────────────┘
│ PostgreSQL :5458         │
│ Redis (internal)         │
└─────────────────────────┘
         │
         ▼ agent-net (shared Docker overlay network)
         │
    Traefik (Swarm)
    meet.paragu-ai.com → dashboard:3000
    api.paragu-ai.com/vexa → api-gateway:8000
```

All services live on the same `agent-net` overlay network used by existing Swarm stacks.

## Components

| Component | Image | Purpose | CPU/GPU | RAM |
|-----------|-------|---------|---------|-----|
| **Speaches** | `ghcr.io/speaches-ai/speaches:latest-cpu` | Whisper ASR server (OpenAI-compatible API) | CPU (int8) | 8GB |
| **Vexa Lite/Compose** | `vexaai/vexa-lite:latest` or full compose | Meeting bot orchestration, dashboard, API | CPU | 4-8GB |
| **MinIO** | `minio/minio:latest` | Recording storage (S3-compatible) | CPU | 1GB+ |
| **PostgreSQL** | `postgres:17-alpine` | Meeting metadata, users, config | CPU | 512MB |

## Speaches Deployment

Speaches provides an OpenAI-compatible `/v1/audio/transcriptions` endpoint using faster-whisper with CTranslate2 (int8 quantization for CPU).

### Setup

```bash
docker run -d --name speaches \
  --restart unless-stopped \
  --cpus 6 \
  --memory 8g \
  -p 127.0.0.1:8030:8000 \
  --network agent-net \
  -v speaches-cache:/root/.cache \
  -e WHISPER__MODEL=Systran/faster-whisper-medium \
  -e WHISPER__COMPUTE_TYPE=int8 \
  -e WHISPER__BEAM_SIZE=1 \
  -e WHISPER__BEST_OF=1 \
  -e WHISPER__VAD_FILTER=true \
  -e ASR_ENGINE=faster_whisper \
  -e HF_HUB_ENABLE_HF_TRANSFER=1 \
  ghcr.io/speaches-ai/speaches:latest-cpu
```

### Model Download (Mandatory First Step)

Speaches requires **explicit model download** before it accepts transcription requests. The model is NOT downloaded on container start — only when you call:

```bash
curl -s -X POST "http://127.0.0.1:8030/v1/models/Systran/faster-whisper-medium"
```

Wait for the download to complete (2.5GB for medium model, ~2-3 min on decent connection). Verify:

```bash
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8030/v1/audio/transcriptions \
  -F "file=@test-speech-en.wav" \
  -F "model=Systran/faster-whisper-medium"
# Expected: {"text":"Hello, this is a test of the transcription service..."}
```

**Model download troubleshooting:**
- If Speaches returns `"Model 'X' is not installed locally"` even after download, the POST to `/v1/models/{model_id}` was not called or failed silently
- The container must have internet access for the initial model download (HuggingFace Hub)
- Model is cached in the mounted volume at `/root/.cache/huggingface/hub/` — persists across restarts

### Model Options (CPU)

| Model | Disk | RAM | Speed (10min audio) | Notes |
|-------|------|-----|---------------------|-------|
| `tiny` / `tiny.en` | 75MB | 250MB | ~30s | Fastest, lower accuracy |
| `base` / `base.en` | 145MB | 500MB | ~1min | Default |
| `small` / `small.en` | 465MB | 1.5GB | ~2min | Good balance |
| `medium` / `medium.en` | 1.5GB | 5GB | ~5min | **Recommended for meetings** |

Use `.en` variants for English-only meetings — faster and more accurate.

## Pricing: What This Replaces

The self-hosted setup replaces these commercial services:

| Commercial Service | Cost | Replaced By |
|-------------------|------|-------------|
| Otter.ai / Fireflies.ai | $10-30/user/mo | **Vexa + Speaches** (free) |
| AgentCall TTS add-on | $8.00/hr | **ElevenLabs TTS** ($0.15/hr) or local |
| Recall.ai | $0.50/hr bot | **Vexa bots** (free, VPS resources) |

**There is no single subscription that covers meeting joining + STT + TTS.** However, Joinly (open-source, MIT, 493 stars) is the closest — see `devops/voice-ai-infrastructure` for Joinly deployment. For the pricing analysis and recommended hybrid architecture (AgentCall Base + ElevenLabs TTS = $0.50/hr vs AgentCall Full $8.47/hr vs Joinly at $0/hr base), see `devops/voice-ai-infrastructure`.

### API Compatibility

Speaches serves an OpenAI-compatible API. Any client that speaks OpenAI's `/v1/audio/transcriptions` endpoint can point here:

```bash
curl http://127.0.0.1:8030/v1/audio/transcriptions \
  -F "file=@recording.mp3" \
  -F "model=Systran/faster-whisper-medium" \
  -F "language=en" \
  -F "response_format=json"
```

Also supports streaming via SSE (`stream=true`) and WebSocket live transcription.

## Vexa Deployment (Full Compose Stack)

Vexa orchestrates meeting bots that join Google Meet/Teams/Zoom, captures audio, and stores recordings.

### Environment (.env)

See `references/vexa-env-config.md` for full .env template.

Key variables:
```
TRANSCRIPTION_SERVICE_URL=http://speaches:8000/v1/audio/transcriptions
TRANSCRIPTION_SERVICE_TOKEN=         # empty for local Speaches
ADMIN_TOKEN=<random-16-char>
STORAGE_BACKEND=minio
```

### Docker Compose Override for Swarm/Traefik Interop

When deploying alongside existing Swarm + Traefik, the Vexa compose stack needs:

1. **All services on `agent-net`** — Traefik's overlay network
2. **Traefik labels** on dashboard and API gateway
3. **Custom ports** to avoid conflicts with existing services

See `references/vexa-traefik-override.md` for the full `docker-compose.override.yml` template.

### Deploying from a Clean Repo Clone

```bash
git clone https://github.com/Vexa-ai/vexa.git /root/vexa-deploy/vexa
cp /root/vexa-deploy/vexa/deploy/env-example /root/vexa-deploy/vexa/.env
# Edit .env with custom values (ports, tokens, etc.)
cp /root/vexa-deploy/vexa/.env /root/vexa-deploy/vexa/deploy/compose/.env
# compose reads .env from the same directory as docker-compose.yml
```

### Critical Env Vars Beyond the Obvious

| Variable | Why It Matters |
|----------|----------------|
| `DOCKER_GID` | Compose mounts `/var/run/docker.sock` and adds group via `group_add`. If the GID on your host differs from the default (998), runtime-api fails with "Permission denied" trying to spawn bot containers. Fix: `stat -c %g /var/run/docker.sock`, set `DOCKER_GID=$GID` in .env |
| `VEXA_ADMIN_API_KEY` | Dashboard uses this to authenticate with admin-api. Must match `ADMIN_TOKEN`. Default in compose is `changeme` — if not overridden, the dashboard shows "Access denied" on sign-in |
| `TRANSCRIPTION_SERVICE_TOKEN` | Must be empty string (not unset) for local Speaches. If it's `=` with no value, some compose parsers set it to empty string which is fine |
| `VEXA_PUBLIC_API_URL` | Dashboard needs to know its public URL for links and redirects — set to `https://meet.paragu-ai.com` |
| `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` | Same as above |

### Post-Deploy Fixup

On first deploy, the admin-api may fail its healthcheck due to a race with postgres initialization. Compose's `depends_on: condition: service_healthy` will fail the entire stack if admin-api is unhealthy at startup. **This is a race, not a real failure:**

```bash
# After docker compose up returns with "dependency failed to start":
docker logs vexa-admin-api-1 2>&1 | tail -5
# If it shows "Application startup complete" / "Uvicorn running", it's healthy now

# Restart the services that depend on it:
docker start vexa-api-gateway-1 vexa-dashboard-1 vexa-mcp-1
```

**Do NOT run `docker compose down -v` for this** — that deletes volumes and forces a full restart. The first-time race is always transient; just start the dependents manually.

### Admin API Password Mismatch After Restart

**Symptom:** `asyncpg.exceptions.InvalidPasswordError: password authentication failed for user "postgres"` even though the .env correctly sets `DB_PASSWORD: [REDACTED] cause:** The compose creates a fresh PostgreSQL container with `POSTGRES_PASSWORD` from the environment. If a previous run used a different password and the volume was NOT deleted (`docker compose down` without `-v`), the old data persists with the old password. The new env var is ignored because PostgreSQL only reads it on initial data dir creation.

**Fix:** If you changed the DB password AND the volume already has data:
```bash
cd /root/vexa-deploy/vexa/deploy/compose
docker compose down -v   # WARNING: destroys ALL data
docker compose up -d     # fresh postgres with new password
```

If you're on a clean deploy (no prior volume), the password IS from the env — check for whitespace or encoding issues in .env.

### Creating Dashboard Users

The email magic link flow requires the dashboard to have the correct `VEXA_ADMIN_API_KEY`. After setting it in .env, recreate the dashboard container:

```bash
cd /root/vexa-deploy/vexa/deploy/compose
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f ../../docker-compose.override.yml up -d dashboard
```

Then sign in at `https://meet.paragu-ai.com` with the admin email. The magic link flow sends a one-time link — but without SMTP configured, it won't deliver email. The workaround is to use the admin API directly:

```bash
# Create user
curl -X POST http://localhost:8056/admin/users \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"email":"[REDACTED_EMAIL]","name":"Ivan"}'

# Create API token
curl -X POST "http://localhost:8056/admin/users/1/tokens" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"dashboard-token"}'

# Set VEXA_API_KEY in .env to the returned token
```

### First-Time Bot Image Setup

Vexa bot containers are **not included** in the compose stack — they're spawned on-demand by `runtime-api` as standalone containers. The images must be pulled explicitly before first use:

```bash
docker pull vexaai/vexa-bot:latest
```

Attempting to send a bot without this image pulled results in a 404 from Docker's HTTP API and a 500 from the meeting API. The compose healthcheck passes because `runtime-api` itself starts fine — the error only appears when you try to create a meeting.

### Key Points for Hybrid Swarm/Compose Networking

- **Standalone Docker compose containers** CAN join a Swarm overlay network (`agent-net`) after you create the network externally, but:
  - The compose project creates its own bridge network — containers must also join the overlay
  - DNS resolution of other overlay containers (like `speaches`) works via Docker's built-in overlay DNS
  - `127.0.0.1` inside a compose container refers to its own network namespace, NOT the host — can't reach host-published ports this way
  - Use `speaches:8000` (service name DNS on agent-net) instead of `127.0.0.1:8030`

- **Port conflicts** are common when deploying alongside 20+ existing Swarm services:
  - Check with: `ss -tlnp "sport = :PORT"` before assigning
  - Common conflicts: 3001 (Evolution API), 9000 (Portainer), 3010 (other Next.js apps)
  - The compose file's `ports:` section must correctly bind to available host ports

- **Traefik label placement** matters for standalone compose:
  - For `docker compose up -d`: labels go at `service.labels:` (NOT `deploy.labels:` — those are Swarm-only)
  - For `docker stack deploy`: labels go at `deploy.labels:`
  - We run Vexa as standalone compose, so use `service.labels:`

### Starting the Stack

```bash
cd /root/vexa-deploy/vexa
# Create agent-net if it doesn't exist (usually it does from Swarm)
docker network create agent-net --driver overlay --attachable 2>/dev/null || true

# Deploy
docker compose -f deploy/compose/docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.override.yml up -d

# Verify
docker compose ps
curl http://localhost:8056/  # API gateway
curl http://localhost:3011/  # Dashboard
```

### Dashboard Access

The dashboard runs on port 3001 (or whatever `DASHBOARD_HOST_PORT` is set to in .env). Behind Traefik at `meet.paragu-ai.com`:
- Login page: create first user via admin API
- Send a bot: paste Google Meet/Teams/Zoom URL
- View transcripts: meeting history page
- Recordings: links in meeting details

### Admin API (First-Time Setup)

After first deploy, create a user and API key. **Auth header for admin API:**
- The compose file sets `admin-api` env: `ADMIN_API_TOKEN=${ADMIN_TOKEN:-changeme}`
- But dashboard reads `VEXA_ADMIN_API_KEY=${ADMIN_API_TOKEN:-changeme}`
- This means if you only set `ADMIN_TOKEN` in .env, dashboard still gets `changeme`

**Fix:** Set ALL THREE in .env:
```
ADMIN_TOKEN=your-token
ADMIN_API_TOKEN=your-token
VEXA_ADMIN_API_KEY=your-token
```

**After updating .env, recreate dashboard:**
```bash
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f ../../docker-compose.override.yml up -d dashboard
```

**Creating users without SMTP (magic link won't deliver):**
The dashboard's email sign-in sends a magic link. Without a mail server configured, the email won't arrive. Create users via admin API first:

```bash
# Create default user
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:8057/admin/users \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"email":"[REDACTED_EMAIL]","name":"Ivan"}'

# Create API token
curl -s "http://localhost:8057/admin/users/1/tokens" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -X POST -d '{"name":"dashboard-token"}'

# Set VEXA_API_KEY in .env to the returned token value
```

Wait — this doesn't work either. The email magic link on the dashboard will still fail even with users created, because the dashboard calls the admin API to send the email and there's no SMTP. **The actual working path is:** set `VEXA_ADMIN_API_KEY` to the admin token (not a user API key), then the dashboard's admin panel lets you manage without email.

## Telegram Delivery Pipeline

After a meeting completes, the pipeline delivers: recording link + transcript + AI summary.

### Architecture

```
Vexa webhook (POST_MEETING_HOOKS) → Python handler
  → Fetch transcript from Speaches API
  → Summarize via Ollama (optional)
  → Generate signed MinIO URL for recording
  → Send Telegram message with links
```

See `templates/telegram-notification-handler.py` for the complete handler template. See also `voice-ai-infrastructure` templates for the ElevenLabs hybrid meeting bot that adds real-time TTS speaking to the recording pipeline.

## Port Allocation Reference

When deploying Vexa alongside existing services, allocate ports that don't conflict:

| Port | Usually Used By | Notes |
|------|-----------------|-------|
| 3001 | Evolution API | Swarm service |
| 3010 | tsuki-test | Standalone Next.js app |
| 5432 | PostgreSQL | Swarm service (internal) |
| 6379 | Redis | Swarm service |
| 9000 | Portainer | Standalone |
| 8030 | Speaches | **Our service** — bind to 127.0.0.1 only |

Vexa compose defaults → custom ports:
- 3001 → 3011 (dashboard — 3010 is tsuki-test)
- 9000 → 9002 (MinIO S3 API)
- 9001 → 9003 (MinIO console)

## Testing Flow

### 1. Verify Speaches Works

```bash
curl -s --max-time 300 http://127.0.0.1:8030/v1/audio/transcriptions \
  -F "file=@test-speech-en.wav" \
  -F "model=Systran/faster-whisper-medium"
```

### 2. Verify Vexa API Is Up

```bash
curl http://localhost:8056/
# Expected: JSON with service status
```

### 3. Send a Bot to a Meeting

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8056/bots \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "X-API-Key: $VEXA_API_KEY" \
  -d '{"platform":"google_meet","native_meeting_id":"abc-defg-hij"}'
```

### 4. Check Dashboard

Open `https://meet.paragu-ai.com` in browser. Login with admin credentials.

## Pitfalls

### 1. Speaches Model Not Downloaded

**Symptom:** Transcription requests return 404 with `"Model not installed locally"`.

**Fix:** Must call `POST /v1/models/{model_id}` explicitly before first transcription. The `WHISPER__MODEL` env var only sets the default — it does NOT trigger download.

### 2. Overlay Network Docker DNS Resolution

**Symptom:** Compose containers can't reach `speaches:8000` even though Speaches is on `agent-net`.

**Fix:** Both Speaches and Vexa containers must be on the SAME overlay network. If Speaches is on `agent-net` and Vexa compose containers only have their internal bridge network, DNS won't resolve. Add each service to `agent-net` via the override.

### 3. Port Conflicts With Existing Services

**Symptom:** `docker compose up` fails with `port is already allocated`.

**Fix:** Check all ports before deploy: `ss -tlnp "sport = :PORT"`. Vexa defaults (3001, 9000) are commonly used by other tools (Evolution API, Portainer).

### 4. Host vs Container Network

**Symptom:** Vexa containers can't reach `127.0.0.1:8030` (Speaches).

**Fix:** Each Docker container has its own `127.0.0.1`. Use the overlay network hostname (`speaches:8000`) instead. The Speaches container must also be on the same overlay network.

### 6. Bot Image Not Found

**Symptom:** Meeting API returns 500 with `"404 Client Error: Not Found for url: http+unix://%2Fvar%2Frun%2Fdocker.sock/containers/create"` in runtime-api logs. Dashboard shows "Failed" meeting status.

**Fix:** The bot image (`vexaai/vexa-bot:latest`) is NOT pulled automatically — it must be pulled manually before first meeting:
```bash
docker pull vexaai/vexa-bot:latest
```
### 7. Real-Time Transcription Fails With Local Speaches

**Symptom:** Vexa dashboard shows "Transcription unavailable" during a live meeting. Bot logs show:
```
error transcribing segment: TypeError: fetch failed
    reason: connect ECONNREFUSED ::1:8000
```

**Root cause:** Vexa's meeting bot sends raw audio segments via HTTP streaming/fetch (raw body, not multipart) to `TRANSCRIPTION_SERVICE_URL`. Speaches expects standard OpenAI-compatible **file upload** (`multipart/form-data POST`) with a `file` field. Protocol mismatch → retry 4 times → fail.

**Effect:**
- ✅ **Recording** — works fine (chunks upload to MinIO)
- ❌ **Live/real-time captions** — broken (audio streaming mismatch)
- ✅ **Post-meeting transcription** — works via the bridge (see below)

### Fix: Transcription Bridge (Deployed and Verified)

The bridge is a lightweight Python HTTP service that translates between Vexa's streaming protocol and Speaches' file-based API. **Deployed, tested, and working.** The webm→wav→Speaches pipeline was verified with real audio from a live meeting chunk.

**Architecture:**
```
Bot audio (raw webm/opus) → transcription-bridge:8031
  → If multipart/form-data: forward directly to Speaches
  → If raw audio: ffmpeg convert to 16kHz mono wav
    → construct multipart → Speaches:8000 → transcript back
```

**Bridge code:** `references/transcription-bridge.py` in this skill.

**Deployment:**
```bash
cd /root/vexa-deploy/bridge
docker build -t transcription-bridge .
docker run -d --name transcription-bridge \
  --restart unless-stopped \
  --network vexa_vexa \
  -p 127.0.0.1:8031:8031 \
  -e SPEACHES_URL=http://speaches:8000 \
  -e MODEL=Systran/faster-whisper-medium \
  transcription-bridge:latest
docker network connect agent-net transcription-bridge
```

**Network requirements:**
- Bridge on `vexa_vexa` (Vexa compose bridge) so spawned bot containers can reach it via DNS
- Bridge ALSO on `agent-net` (overlay) so it can resolve `speaches:8000`
- If the bot tries to reach Speaches directly (bypassing bridge), Speaches must be isolated to prevent that — move Speaches to a separate `whisper-net` that only the bridge can access

**Isolating Speaches from bot containers (critical for built-in transcription to route through bridge):**
```bash
docker network create whisper-net
docker network disconnect agent-net speaches
docker network connect whisper-net speaches
docker network connect whisper-net transcription-bridge
```

Without this isolation, the bot may resolve `speaches:8000` directly (if both are on `agent-net`) and bypass the bridge entirely, resulting in the same streaming fetch failure.

**Symptom of bot bypassing bridge:** Speaches logs show 404 requests coming from the bot's IP (e.g., `10.0.2.33`) rather than from the bridge's IP. The bridge logs show the bot connecting but returning 502 ("Speaches error: HTTP Error 404: Not Found").

**Note: Bridge handles multipart forwarding AND raw audio conversion.** Two code paths:
1. If the bot sends `multipart/form-data` → bridge forwards directly to Speaches (pass-through)
2. If the bot sends raw audio (webm/opus body) → bridge: ffmpeg convert to 16kHz mono wav → construct multipart → Speaches → return transcript

Both paths are tested and working. The raw audio path takes ~30s for a 30s audio chunk (ffmpeg + transcription).

**Configure Vexa to use the bridge:**
```bash
# In .env:
TRANSCRIPTION_SERVICE_URL=http://transcription-bridge:8031/v1/audio/transcriptions
# Recreate meeting-api and runtime-api so they pass this to newly spawned bots:
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f ../../docker-compose.override.yml up -d meeting-api runtime-api
```

**Testing the bridge:**
```bash
# Test raw webm/opus conversion path (what the bot sends):
ffmpeg -y -i test-speech-en.wav -c:a libopus -b:a 16k -ar 16000 -ac 1 test-audio.webm
curl -s --max-time 600 -X POST "http://localhost:8031/v1/audio/transcriptions" \
  -H "Content-Type: audio/webm" \
  --data-binary @test-audio.webm

# Test multipart forwarding path (pass-through to Speaches):
curl -s --max-time 600 -X POST "http://localhost:8031/v1/audio/transcriptions" \
  -F "file=@test-speech-en.wav" \
  -F "model=Systran/faster-whisper-medium"
```

### 7b. Vexa Bot Container Network Attachment

When `runtime-api` spawns a bot container, it attaches to the **compose project bridge network** (e.g., `vexa_vexa`), NOT to any overlay network. This means:

- The bot CANNOT resolve hostnames on overlay networks like `agent-net` unless those services are also on the compose bridge
- The bot CAN resolve other compose services by name (e.g., `transcription-bridge:8031`)  
- The bot CANNOT reach `127.0.0.1:PORT` on the host — each container has its own loopback

**Implication for the bridge:** The bot can reach `transcription-bridge:8031` only if the bridge container is also on `vexa_vexa`. The bridge in turn needs to reach `speaches:8000` — this requires attaching the bridge to BOTH networks:
```bash
docker network connect vexa_vexa transcription-bridge  # bot can reach bridge
docker network connect agent-net transcription-bridge   # bridge can reach speaches
```

**Race condition on container creation:** If the runtime-api cannot reach the Docker socket (wrong `DOCKER_GID`), it silently fails to spawn bots. The compose passes `group_add` and mounts the socket — but if the host's Docker GID doesn't match the container's expectation, the error appears as a 500 from meeting-api's internal Docker HTTP call. Fix: `stat -c %g /var/run/docker.sock` → set `DOCKER_GID=$GID` in .env → `docker compose up -d runtime-api`.

### 7c. Bot's Voice Agent (TTS/Speaking) Works Without Configuration

Vexa bots can speak during meetings. The compose stack ships a TTS service (`tts-service:8002`) using Piper (local voice synthesis). The bot automatically:
- Listens with PulseAudio virtual microphone
- Speaks via text-to-speech when triggered
- Mutes/unmutes itself dynamically

This is configured by default and requires no additional setup. The bot's `OPENAI_API_KEY` env can be set for higher-quality OpenAI TTS voices, but Piper works out of the box with zero configuration.

### 7c. Meeting Flow (End-to-End)

When you click "Start Transcription" or call the API:

1. **Bot creation:** `meeting-api` calls `runtime-api` which spawns a Docker container (`vexaai/vexa-bot:latest`) as a child process
2. **Network attachment:** The bot container joins the compose project network (e.g. `vexa_vexa`) — not the overlay network
3. **Browser launch:** The bot runs a headless Chromium instance via Playwright
4. **Meeting join:** Navigates to the Google Meet/Teams/Zoom URL
5. **Lobby detection:** If a waiting room is detected, polls every 2s for up to 15 minutes
6. **Admission:** Once admitted, the bot:
   - Starts MediaRecorder (`audio/webm;codecs=opus`, 30s chunks)
   - Uploads chunks to MinIO via the meeting-api's `/internal/recordings/upload` endpoint
   - Spawns per-speaker transcription clients that send audio segments to `transcription-bridge:8031`
   - (Optional) TTS pipeline for speaking — Piper or OpenAI voice via `tts-service`
7. **Meeting end:** The bot exits when all participants leave, or on timeout, or via dashboard cancel
8. **Post-processing:** Recordings stored as `.webm` chunks in MinIO at `recordings/{meeting_id}/{session_uid}/audio/*.webm`
9. **Transcription:** The bridge converts each chunk and sends to Speaches. Transcripts appear in dashboard "Transcript" tab

### 8. .env File Placement

**Symptom:** `docker compose up` doesn't pick up variables from your .env.

**Fix:** Docker Compose reads .env from the SAME directory as the docker-compose.yml file. If your compose file is at `deploy/compose/docker-compose.yml`, place .env at `deploy/compose/.env`, NOT at the repo root.

### 9. Dashboard Auth Token Propagation

**Symptom:** Dashboard shows "Access denied" or sign-in loop even with correct admin tokens in .env.

**Root cause:** The Vexa compose file reads these env vars for the dashboard, but they have DIFFERENT names than the admin API ones:
- `admin-api` reads `ADMIN_API_TOKEN=${ADMIN_TOKEN:-changeme}` (indirect — uses ADMIN_TOKEN)
- `dashboard` reads `VEXA_ADMIN_API_KEY=${ADMIN_API_TOKEN:-changeme}` (direct — uses ADMIN_API_TOKEN)
- So if you set `ADMIN_TOKEN=xxx` but NOT `ADMIN_API_TOKEN=xxx`, the dashboard falls back to `changeme`

**Fix:** Set ALL in .env:
```
ADMIN_TOKEN=your-token
ADMIN_API_TOKEN=your-token
VEXA_ADMIN_API_KEY=your-token
```

Then recreate the dashboard container. The dashboard's JWT/email-auth flow may still not work without SMTP — the admin panel (`Unlock Admin`) bypasses email auth entirely.

### 10. Traefik Label Placement for Standalone Compose

**Symptom:** Traefik labels work with `docker stack deploy` (Swarm) but are ignored by `docker compose up -d` (standalone).

**Fix:** Place labels at `service.labels:` for standalone compose (NOT under `deploy:`). Verify with `docker inspect $CID --format '{{json .Config.Labels}}'`.

## Skill Boundary

This skill owns: **recording + transcription + storage** (Vexa meeting bots, Speaches Whisper ASR, MinIO, Telegram delivery). It is the "bot joins the meeting and captures everything" layer.

For the **AI talking layer** (LiveKit voice agents, AgentCall participation, ElevenLabs Conversational AI, STT-LLM-TTS pipelines), see `devops/voice-ai-infrastructure`. That skill owns the real-time conversational AI and the hybrid pipeline where the AI listens AND speaks in meetings. As of May 2026, **Joinly** (single Docker image, open-source, MIT) is the recommended stack — replaces AgentCall entirely for the talking layer. See the Joinly section in that skill.

For the **complete meeting bot with context + memory + delivery** (Joinly + Hermes MCP + Supermemory + Telegram), see the "Full Hermes Meeting Bot Pipeline" section in `devops/voice-ai-infrastructure`. All files at `/root/vexa-deploy/joinly/`.

Together they cover the full stack:
```
self-hosted-meeting-infrastructure       voice-ai-infrastructure
┌─────────────────────────┐             ┌─────────────────────┐
│ Vexa joins meeting       │             │ AgentCall/ElevenLabs│
│ Speaches transcribes     │────audio──▶│ AI thinks & responds │
│ MinIO stores recordings  │             │ ElevenLabs TTS talks │
│ Telegram delivers        │             │ LiveKit voice chat   │
└─────────────────────────┘             └─────────────────────┘

> **⚠️ Overlap notice:** This skill is the user-facing/operations view. For detailed deployment steps (env vars, Traefik wiring, Speaches setup, port allocation, credential propagation, hybrid networking), see `devops/self-hosted-meeting-infrastructure`. For voice-to-voice AI agents and meeting participation (LiveKit, AgentCall talking bots), see `devops/voice-ai-infrastructure`. All three skills are maintained until the curator absorbs them.

Deploy a self-hosted meeting bot that joins client calls, records audio, transcribes, summarizes via LLM, and delivers links through Hermes messaging channels.

## Architecture

```
User sends "meet at <url>" via Telegram/WhatsApp
        │
        v
Hermes calls Vexa API (or runs `hermes meet join`)
        │
        v
Bot joins meeting as participant → captures audio
        │
        v
Recording → MinIO/S3 (on VPS)
Transcription → Whisper API (cloud or local GPU)
        │
        v
Post-processing:
  - Ollama summarizes transcript
  - Generates shareable HTML page
        │
        v
Delivery: send_message with recording link + transcript + summary
```

## Three-Layer Stack

| Layer | Tool | Notes |
|-------|------|-------|
| Bot (joins meetings) | **Vexa** or `hermes meet` | Vexa: multi-platform (Meet/Teams/Zoom). Hermes: Google Meet only, no recording. |
| Transcription | **Speaches** (faster-whisper) or Vexa cloud API | CPU-only: medium model ~2-5 min for 10-min. GPU needed for real-time. |
| Delivery | Custom script via Hermes tools | Watch Vexa webhook → format → send_message to Telegram/WhatsApp |

## Platform Support

| Platform | Vexa | Hermes Meet |
|----------|------|-------------|
| Google Meet | ✅ Full | ✅ Captions only, no recording |
| Microsoft Teams | ✅ Full | ❌ |
| Zoom | ✅ (via web) | ❌ |

## Deployment Options

### Option A: Vexa Lite (single container) — RECOMMENDED FOR TRIAL

Single container with 14 services. Needs external PostgreSQL + transcription API key.

```bash
git clone https://github.com/Vexa-ai/vexa.git && cd vexa
cp deploy/env-example .env
# Edit .env: set TRANSCRIPTION_SERVICE_URL and TRANSCRIPTION_SERVICE_TOKEN
make lite
```

Services inside container:
- API Gateway :8056, Dashboard :3000, Redis, Xvfb, PulseAudio
- Bots spawn as child processes

Get transcription token at https://vexa.ai/account (free tier available).

### Option B: Vexa Full Stack (Docker Compose)

Each service runs in its own container. Requires PostgreSQL, Redis, MinIO.

```bash
cd vexa/deploy/compose
make all
```

Better for production: bot isolation per container, persistent Redis, S3-compatible storage.

### Option C: Hermes Meet (Google Meet only, no recording)

Built into Hermes Agent — no new containers needed.

```bash
hermes meet setup          # installs Chromium + playwright
hermes meet auth           # Google OAuth
hermes meet join https://meet.google.com/abc-defg-hij
hermes meet transcript      # get captions
hermes meet stop            # leave
```

**Limitation:** Captions only — no audio/video recording. Use for quick text transcription.

## Infrastructure Prerequisites

- Docker + Docker Compose on VPS
- PostgreSQL (can run as container on same VPS)
- For full recording: MinIO or S3-compatible storage
- Traefik reverse proxy (already running) for HTTPS
- 8GB+ RAM minimum, 16GB+ recommended for concurrent meetings
- No GPU required if using Vexa cloud transcription

## VPS Resource Usage

Based on Hostinger KVM 8 (8 vCPU, 32GB RAM):
- Vexa Lite with 1 concurrent bot: ~2-4GB RAM
- Vexa full stack: ~4-6GB RAM
- Speaches (CPU transcription, medium model): ~2GB RAM per request
- Each additional concurrent bot: ~1-2GB

## Known Pitfalls

- **Transcription requires GPU for local real-time.** On CPU, use Vexa cloud API or accept post-meeting batch transcription.
- **Zoom platform support** may require Zoom Marketplace app approval for joining others' meetings.
- **Docker-in-Docker:** Vexa Compose mode needs Docker socket access for bot containers.
- **Bot zombies:** Vexa Lite may leave zombie processes; restart container periodically.
- **Recording storage:** Default is local (`/var/lib/vexa/recordings`). Configure MinIO/S3 for durability.

## Delivery Pipeline (Post-Meeting)

After a meeting ends, Vexa can fire webhooks. Build a small consumer that:

1. Receives meeting-complete webhook
2. Downloads transcript from Vexa API
3. Emits transcript to Ollama for summarization
4. Generates a clean HTML page with recording player + transcript + summary
5. Makes page accessible via Traefik subdomain (e.g., `meet.paragu-ai.com/recording/<id>`)
6. Sends link via `send_message` to user's Telegram/WhatsApp

## Quick Start Checklist

- [ ] Decide platform(s): Google Meet only (Hermes) or multi-platform (Vexa)
- [ ] Deploy Vexa Lite or full stack
- [ ] Configure Traefik rules for API + dashboard + recordings
- [ ] Test with `curl -X POST /bots` to join a real meeting
- [ ] Build delivery pipeline for recording links + summaries

## Three Modes of Meeting AI (Comparison)

| Mode | Tool | What It Does | Cost | Can Talk? |
|------|------|-------------|------|-----------|
| **Record & Transcribe** | Vexa + Speaches | Bot joins, records audio, transcribes post-meeting | Free (VPS) | ❌ |
| **Live Transcribe** | Vexa + transcription-bridge + Speaches | Bot joins, streams audio, transcribes in real-time via bridge | Free (VPS) | ❌ |
| **Voice-to-Voice** | LiveKit + Deepgram + ElevenLabs | One-on-one voice conversation with AI via WebRTC | API keys | ✅ |
| **Meeting Participant** | AgentCall | AI joins meeting, speaks, listens, does interviews | $0.35/hr | ✅ |
| **Captions Only** | Hermes `meet join` | Headless Chromium scrapes captions, no recording | Free | ❌ |

**Note: AgentCall vs Hermes Meet are different tools.** Hermes' built-in `meet join` command uses headless Chromium and scrapes captions — listen-only, Google Meet only, no talking. AgentCall uses a WebSocket bridge to their cloud service — the bot joins as a real participant, speaks, listens, and handles interruptions. They are independent and solve different problems.

# Voice AI Infrastructure

Class-level skill for deploying real-time voice AI: LiveKit (WebRTC server + voice agents) and AgentCall (AI meeting participant). Together they enable voice conversations between humans and AI agents — from one-on-one chat to multi-party meeting participation.

## Architecture

```
Your Device (mic + speaker)
    │
    ├── WebRTC ── LiveKit Server (:7880)
    │                 │
    │                 ├── Hermes Voice Agent (STT→LLM→TTS)
    │                 │     ├── Deepgram (STT)
    │                 │     ├── ElevenLabs (TTS)
    │                 │     └── OpenAI Realtime (LLM)
    │                 │
    │                 └── (future: more agents)
    │
    └── Browser ── AgentCall MCP (:stdio)
                      │
                      ├── Join Google Meet/Zoom/Teams
                      ├── Speak/listen/interrupt
                      └── Base $0.35/hr (TTS add-on $8/hr)
```

## Components

| Component | Purpose | Provider | Cost | Self-Hostable |
|-----------|---------|----------|------|---------------|
| **LiveKit Server** | WebRTC signaling, room management, TURN | Open source (Apache 2.0) | Free | ✅ Yes |
| **Hermes Voice Agent** | STT→LLM→TTS pipeline | Python + LiveKit SDK | Free | ✅ Yes |
| **Deepgram** | Speech-to-text (nova-3 model) | Cloud API | $0.46/hr or $200 free credit | ❌ No |
| **ElevenLabs** | Text-to-speech (multilingual v2) | Cloud API | ~$0.15/hr (10k chars/mo free) | ❌ No |
| **AgentCall** | AI participant in meetings | Cloud service | $0.35/hr base | ❌ No |
| **[Planned] Self-Hosted Pipeline** | Vexa + Deepgram + ElevenLabs | Your infra | ~$0.61/hr | ✅ |

## Pricing Comparison: Which Service Covers Everything?

**User preference (Ivan):** "I'd rather pay only 1 subscription" — wants a single service that covers bot meeting join + STT + TTS.

### The Short Answer

**No single subscription covers all three.** Here's why:

| If you subscribe to... | It covers | Missing |
|------------------------|-----------|---------|
| **ElevenLabs Pro $99/mo** | STT + TTS + AI agent + 100 chars/mo | ❌ Can't join Google Meet |
| **Deepgram $200 credit** | STT only (~430 free hours) | ❌ No TTS, no meeting join |
| **AgentCall Base $0.35/hr** | Meeting join + listen | ❌ No TTS (+$8/hr extra) |
| **AgentCall Full $8.47/hr** | Everything | ✅ But expensive |

### Service Coverage Matrix

| Capability | Deepgram | ElevenLabs | AgentCall |
|-----------|----------|------------|-----------|
| **STT** (listen to speech) | ✅ Best-in-class (nova-3) | ✅ Scribe (competitive model) | ❌ Uses Deepgram under the hood |
| **TTS** (speak back) | ❌ | ✅ World's best quality | ✅ Kokoro (basic, open-source) |
| **Bot joins Google Meet** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Only service that does this |
| **Bot joins Zoom/Teams** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Only service that does this |
| **Conversational AI agent** | ❌ | ✅ ElevenAgents (full platform, $6/hr) | ✅ Bridge + MCP tools |
| **Screenshare** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Included | 
| **Meeting chat** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Bidirectional |

### The AgentCall TTS Problem

AgentCall charges **$8/hr** for TTS. The TTS engine is **Kokoro** — an open-source model that runs on CPU. Compare:

| Service | Hourly Cost | Quality |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **AgentCall TTS** | **$8.00** | Kokoro (basic, open-source) |
| **ElevenLabs Flash** | **~$0.15** | Best-in-world, $0.05/1K chars |
| **ElevenLabs Multilingual v2** | **~$0.30** | Premium, $0.10/1K chars |

AgentCall TTS is **53x more expensive** than ElevenLabs for lower quality. Never pay for AgentCall TTS.

### Cost Per Hour — Meeting Bot That Talks

| Strategy | Breakdown | Total/hr | Notes |
|----------|-----------|----------|-------|
| **AgentCall Full** | Base $0.35 + TTS add-on $8.00 | **$8.47** | ✅ Everything, but waste of money |
| **AgentCall Base + ElevenLabs** | Base $0.35 + ElevenLabs $0.15 | **$0.50** | ✅ Best value — use ElevenLabs for TTS |
| **Deepgram + ElevenLabs + Vexa** | STT $0.46 + TTS $0.15, join is free | **$0.61** | ✅ Fully self-hosted (WIP — needs audio relay) |
| **ElevenAgents** | $0.10/min = $6/hr | **$6.00** | STT+TTS+LLM all-in-one, no meeting join |

### Recommended Architecture

**Publishable as the "default recommendation" for this user:**

```
1. Bot joins meeting → AgentCall Base ($0.35/hr) OR Vexa (free)
2. Hears client → Deepgram STT ($0.46/hr, often free with credits)
3. Decides response → Your LLM (free via Groq/Hermes)
4. Speaks back → ElevenLabs TTS ($0.15/hr)
```

**Total operating cost: $0.50-$0.61/hr** — versus $8.47/hr for AgentCall's full stack. The saving comes from replacing AgentCall's marked-up TTS with ElevenLabs' direct API.

**If you want a truly single-subscription experience with no assembly required:** AgentCall Full at $8.47/hr works, but you pay for the convenience of not integrating ElevenLabs yourself. Given you already have both API keys set up, the integration is ~2-3 hours of work.

## LiveKit Server Deployment

### Docker Compose (Standalone, Behind Traefik)

```yaml
name: livekit

services:
  livekit-server:
    image: livekit/livekit-server:latest
    environment:
      LIVEKIT_KEYS: "api-key: api-secret"
      LIVEKIT_REDIS_HOST: "redis:6379"
      LIVEKIT_PORT: "7880"
      LIVEKIT_UDP_PORT: "7881"
      LIVEKIT_RTC_PORT_RANGE_START: "7882"
      LIVEKIT_RTC_PORT_RANGE_END: "7890"
      LIVEKIT_TURN_ENABLED: "false"
      LIVEKIT_LOGGING_LEVEL: "info"
    ports:
      - "7880:7880"
      - "7881:7881/udp"
      - "[REDACTED_PHONE]:[REDACTED_PHONE]/udp"
    networks:
      - agent-net
    labels:
      - "traefik.enable=true"
      - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net"
      - "traefik.http.routers.livekit-api.entrypoints=websecure"
      - "traefik.http.routers.livekit-api.rule=Host(`livekit.paragu-ai.com`)"
      - "traefik.http.routers.livekit-api.tls=true"
      - "traefik.http.routers.livekit-api.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver"
      - "traefik.http.services.livekit-api.loadbalancer.server.port=7880"

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    command: redis-server --appendonly yes --save 60 1
    networks:
      - agent-net
```

### Networking Notes

- **WebRTC needs direct UDP** — Cloudflare proxied DNS (orange cloud) will NOT work for voice. The `livekit` A record MUST be DNS-only (gray cloud).
- The server exposes ports 7880 (HTTP/WS), 7881 (UDP ICE), and [REDACTED_PHONE] (RTC).
- TURN requires a valid TLS cert — disable with `LIVEKIT_TURN_ENABLED=false` for testing without one.

### Verify Server is Running

```bash
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:7880/
# Expected: OK
```

The root `/` returns `OK` — no JSON, no HTML. This is normal. The WebSocket signaling endpoint is at `/rtc`.

## Voice Agent (Python, LiveKit Agents SDK v1.5+)

The Hermes voice agent is a Python program using `livekit-agents` SDK. It connects to the LiveKit server, listens for room sessions, and runs the STT→LLM→TTS loop.

### Agent Script Structure

```python
import os
from livekit.agents import Agent, AgentSession, JobContext, WorkerOptions, cli, function_tool
from livekit.plugins import silero, deepgram, elevenlabs, openai

DG_KEY = os.environ.get("DEEPGRAM_API_KEY", "")
EL_KEY = os.environ.get("ELEVENLABS_API_KEY", "")

@function_tool
async def ask_hermes(context: RunContext, message: str):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=120) as client:
        resp = await client.post("http://hermes:8080/chat", json={"message": message})
        return resp.json().get("response", "Got a response.")

async def entrypoint(ctx: JobContext):
    session = AgentSession(
        vad=silero.VAD.load(),
        stt=deepgram.STT(model="nova-2-general", language="en", api_key=DG_KEY),
        tts=elevenlabs.TTS(model="eleven_multilingual_v2", voice="21m00Tcm4TlvDq8ikWAM", api_key=EL_KEY),
        llm=openai.realtime.RealtimeModel(model="gpt-4o-realtime-preview", voice="alloy"),
        turn_detection=openai.realtime.TurnDetector(),
    )
    agent = Agent(instructions="...", tools=[ask_hermes])
    await session.start(agent=agent, room=ctx.room)
    await session.generate_reply(instructions="Greet the user...")
    await session.wait_for_end()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    cli.run_app(WorkerOptions(entrypoint_fnc=entrypoint))
```

### Dockerfile

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir \
    livekit-agents[deepgram,elevenlabs,silero,openai]~=1.5 \
    httpx
COPY agent.py .
CMD ["python", "agent.py"]
```

### API VERSION NOTES (CRITICAL)

**LiveKit Agents SDK v1.5+ changed the API:**

| Old API (v1.4) | New API (v1.5+) | Notes |
|----------------|-----------------|-------|
| `AgentServer(entrypoint_fnc=fn)` | `cli.run_app(WorkerOptions(entrypoint_fnc=fn))` | Server object removed |
| `@server.rtc_session()` decorator | No decorator | Just pass function directly |
| `from livekit.agents import AgentServer` | `from livekit.agents import WorkerOptions` | Import changes |

**Pitfall:** Attempting to use `AgentServer(entrypoint_fnc=fn)` with v1.5+ gives:
```
TypeError: AgentServer.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'entrypoint_fnc'
```

### Service Registration

When the agent starts, it logs:
```json
{"message": "registered worker", "agent_name": "", "id": "AW_xxxx"}
```

This means it's connected to the LiveKit server and ready to accept room sessions.

### Cartesia TTS Fallback

If the ElevenLabs API key is not available, fall back to Cartesia (free 30min tier):
```python
tts_provider = cartesia.TTS(model="sonic-2", voice="9626c31c-bec5-4cca-baa8-f8ba9e84c8bc")
```

Requires: `livekit-agents[cartesia]~=1.5`

## API Keys Required

| Provider | Purpose | Sign Up | Free Tier |
|----------|---------|---------|-----------|
| **Deepgram** | Speech-to-text (nova-3) | console.deepgram.com | $200 credit |
| **ElevenLabs** | Text-to-speech (multilingual v2) | elevenlabs.io | 10k chars/mo |

Both keys go into the agent's environment, set in the compose `.env` file.

## AgentCall Setup (AI Meeting Participant)

AgentCall is a commercial service ($0.35/hr base) that lets an AI join Google Meet/Zoom/Teams as a participant. It handles the hard parts: real-time voice, barge-in prevention, interruption handling, screen share detection.

### As MCP Server in Hermes

AgentCall exposes 21 tools via MCP. To connect Hermes to AgentCall:

1. **Get an API key:** https://app.agentcall.dev/api-keys (format: `ak_ac_xxxxxxxx`)
2. **Run the MCP server** as a Docker container:
```yaml
  agentcall-mcp:
    image: node:20-slim
    command: sh -c "npx -y @agentcall/mcp-server"
    environment:
      AGENTCALL_API_KEY: "${AGENTCALL_API_KEY}"
    networks:
      - agent-net
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
```
3. **Add to Hermes config.yaml:**
```yaml
env:
  AGENTCALL_API_KEY: ak_ac_xxxx
mcp_servers:
  agentcall:
    transport: stdio
    command: docker
    args:
      - exec
      - "-i"
      - livekit-agentcall-mcp-1
      - npx
      - "-y"
      - "@agentcall/mcp-server"
```

**Note:** The MCP server uses STDIO transport. `docker exec -i` (interactive mode, no TTY) is required.

### AgentCall Bridge Usage — Interactive Session Required

**Critical:** The `bridge.py` script requires interactive stdin to send TTS commands. If you start it with `> logfile 2>&1` or its stdin is `/dev/null`, the bot joins the meeting but **never speaks**. The bot CAN hear participants (transcripts appear on stdout) but CANNOT respond.

**Correct usage (enables speaking):**
```bash
cd /root/vexa-deploy/agentcall-meet/scripts/python
AGENTCALL_API_KEY=ak_ac_xxxx python3 bridge.py "https://meet.google.com/xxx" --name "Hermes" --voice "en_us_001"
# Type: tts.speak "Hello, this is a test message"
```

**Incorrect usage (bot joins but stays silent):**
```bash
# Process started as background job — stdin = /dev/null
AGENTCALL_API_KEY=ak_ac_xxxx python3 bridge.py "https://meet.google.com/xxx" > log.txt 2>&1 &
# ^^ Bot joins, hears everyone, but never speaks
```

**Stdin check:**
```bash
ls -la /proc/$PID/fd/0
# /dev/null  → bot is deaf-mute (can hear, can't speak)
# /dev/pts/0 → bot can speak (interactive terminal)
```

**Troubleshooting checklist for silent bot:**
1. Check stdin: `ls -la /proc/$PID/fd/0`
2. Check API key prefix: must start with `ac_live_`
3. Check concurrent calls: `GET /v1/calls` — free plan allows 1 at a time
4. Wait ~2s after deleting a stale call before creating a new one
5. Telltale sign: stdout shows `greeting.prompt` / `user.message` events but no speak command is ever sent

### Managing Active Calls (API)

```bash
# List active calls
curl -s 'https://api.agentcall.dev/v1/calls' -H 'Authorization: Bearer ak_ac_xxxx'

# End a call (force cleanup)
curl -s -X DELETE "https://api.agentcall.dev/v1/calls/{call_id}" -H 'Authorization: Bearer ak_ac_xxxx'

# Send TTS via API
curl -s -X PUT "https://api.agentcall.dev/v1/calls/{call_id}" \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer ak_ac_xxxx' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"action":"set_user_input","text":"tts.speak Hello world"}'

# Direct WebSocket control
ws_url = f"wss://api.agentcall.dev/v1/calls/{call_id}/ws?api_key={API_KEY}"
# The WebSocket receives events (transcripts, participant join/leave) and accepts speak commands
```

### Concurrent Call Limit

Free plan: **1 concurrent call**. Attempting a second returns 429. Check before creating.

### AgentCall vs Hermes Built-in Meet Tool

| Feature | Hermes `meet join` | AgentCall `bridge.py` |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Platform | Google Meet only | Google Meet, Teams, Zoom |
| Can hear | ✅ Captions | ✅ Full audio STT |
| Can speak | ❌ No | ✅ TTS via stdin |
| Can screen share | ❌ No | ✅ Optional |
| Cost | Free (VPS) | $0.35/hr |
| Needs API key | No | Yes (ak_ac_xxx) |

## LiveKit Config: CLI Flags vs YAML

**v1.11.0 does NOT support YAML config files** — use environment variables only. YAML-based config fails with:
```
could not parse config: yaml: unmarshal errors:
  line 2: field bind not found in type config.Config
```

| Config Item | Env Var | Default |
|-------------|---------|---------|
| Port | `LIVEKIT_PORT` | 7880 |
| UDP port | `LIVEKIT_UDP_PORT` | 7881 |
| RTC port range | `LIVEKIT_RTC_PORT_RANGE_START` / `_END` | [REDACTED_PHONE] |
| Redis | `LIVEKIT_REDIS_HOST` | (none, required) |
| API keys | `LIVEKIT_KEYS` | (none, required) |
| TURN enabled | `LIVEKIT_TURN_ENABLED` | true |
| TURN domain | `LIVEKIT_TURN_DOMAIN` | (none) |
| Log level | `LIVEKIT_LOGGING_LEVEL` | info |

Use env vars from docker-compose; do NOT mount a config file.

## DNS Setup

`livekit.paragu-ai.com` → A record pointing to VPS IP. **Must be DNS-only (gray cloud).** Cloudflare proxied mode breaks WebRTC UDP connections.

## Pitfalls

### 1. AgentCall MCP Service First Launch
First launch takes ~30s to download dependencies. The container logs:
```
\|/-\\|/-\\\\|/- // spinner // AgentCall MCP server running (21 tools, 4 prompts registered)
```

### 2. API Key Masking in Terminal Output
Use Python to inspect Docker envs when the credential masker hides values:
```bash
docker inspect container | python3 -c "import sys,json; c=json.load(sys.stdin)[0]; print([x for x in c['Config']['Env'] if 'ADMIN' in x])"
```

### 3. Key Length Warning for LiveKit
```
InsecureKeyLengthWarning: The HMAC key is 23 bytes long...
```
Not harmful in dev. Use 32+ bytes for production.

### 4. WebRTC Requires UDP Ports Open
Firewall must allow UDP on ports [REDACTED_PHONE].

### 5. No Built-in LiveKit Test UI
Use `livekit-cli` (`pip install livekit-cli`) to join a room from terminal, or use the LiveKit React SDK in a browser.

### 6. AgentCall TTS Cost
**Never pay for AgentCall's $8/hr TTS add-on.** Use ElevenLabs directly ($0.15/hr). See pricing analysis above.

## Joinly — Open-Source Alternative (Replaces AgentCall Entirely)

[joinly.ai](https://github.com/joinly-ai/joinly) — **493 stars, MIT license.** Single Docker image that joins Google Meet/Zoom/Teams and lets the AI both listen AND speak. Replaces the entire AgentCall + ElevenLabs hybrid with one container.

### Key Advantages Over AgentCall

| | Joinly (open-source) | AgentCall (commercial) |
|--|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Cost** | **$0** (VPS resources) | $0.35/hr base + TTS |
| **STT** | Whisper (local, free) or Deepgram API | Uses Deepgram under hood |
| **TTS** | Kokoro (local, free) or ElevenLabs API | Kokoro at $8/hr markup |
| **LLM** | Any (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, DeepSeek) | Their choice |
| **Joins** | Google Meet, Zoom, Teams | Same |
| **Interruptions** | ✅ Built-in turn detection | ✅ Barge-in prevention |
| **MCP support** | ✅ Full MCP server for code tools | ✅ MCP tools |
| **Docker** | ✅ `docker run` single command | Separate bridge script |
| **License** | MIT (Free) | Proprietary ($0.35/hr) |

### Quick Deploy

```bash
docker run --rm --network host \
  --env-file .env \
  ghcr.io/joinly-ai/joinly:latest \
  --client "https://meet.google.com/abc-defg-hij" \
  --name "Hermes" \
  --tts elevenlabs \
  --tts-arg voice_id=21m00Tcm4TlvDq8ikWAM \
  --stt deepgram \
  --stt-arg model_name=nova-2-general \
  -v
```

### .env File

```env
# LLM (use any OpenAI-compatible provider — DeepSeek is free)
JOINLY_LLM_MODEL=deepseek-chat
JOINLY_LLM_PROVIDER=openai
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-your-key
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://api.deepseek.com/v1

# STT
DEEPGRAM_API_KEY=your-deepgram-key

# TTS
ELEVENLABS_API_KEY=your-elevenlabs-key
```

### Architecture

```bash
docker run --network host ghcr.io/joinly-ai/joinly:latest --client <URL>
                                        │
                                        ▼
                          Joinly Container (single process)
                    ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
                    │  Headless Chromium (Playwright)  │
                    │  → Joins Google Meet/Zoom/Teams │
                    │  → Captures browser audio       │
                    │  → Plays TTS audio back          │
                    │                                  │
                    │  STT → LLM → TTS Pipeline        │
                    │  (configurable providers)        │
                    │                                  │
                    │  Turn Detection                  │
                    │  → Interruption handling         │
                    │  → Natural conversation flow     │
                    └─────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Joinly Fields Tested (Live Meeting, May 10 2026)

**Successfully tested in a real Google Meet at `cvh-sprr-mba`.** The bot:
1. ✅ Joined the meeting as "Hermes"
2. ✅ **Spoke first**: "Hi Steve, this is Hermes. I'm here to help out."
3. ✅ Heard the user: "ivan weiss van der pol: I am Ivan."
4. ✅ **Handled interruption**: When interrupted mid-sentence by detected speech, Joinly stopped speaking and waited
5. ✅ **Corrected itself**: "Got it, Ivan! Sorry for the mix-up. How can I help you today?"
6. ✅ Maintained a full conversation flow: the AI asked about client needs, explained its process\n7. ✅ **Responded in real-time** through the meeting speakers\n\n**Pitfall: `--prompt` vs `--system-prompt`.** Joinly uses `--prompt` (not `--system-prompt`). Shell escaping multi-line prompts works but flags are case-sensitive. Using `--system-prompt` gives: `Error: No such option: --system-prompt Did you mean --prompt?`

**Stack used:**
- Joinly Docker image (ghcr.io/joinly-ai/joinly:latest)
- Deepgram STT (nova-2-general via `--stt deepgram`)
- DeepSeek LLM (free, via `OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://api.deepseek.com/v1`)
- ElevenLabs TTS (Rachel voice via `--tts elevenlabs --tts-arg voice_id=21m00Tcm4TlvDq8ikWAM`)

**Notable observations from the live test:**
- The greeting was for "Steve" instead of "Ivan" — the LLM hallucinated the name. This is a prompt engineering issue, not a platform limitation.
- **Interruption handling works naturally** — Joinly uses VAD (voice activity detection) and stops speaking when the user starts talking
- The `.env` file must include `OPENAI_BASE_URL` for DeepSeek compatibility (Joinly uses the OpenAI client library)
- Deepgram key, ElevenLabs key, and OpenAI key (or compatible) are required at minimum
- The `--network host` flag is required for PulseAudio virtual microphone to function inside the container
- CPU-only — no GPU needed for this stack (Whisper STT runs on CPU, but Deepgram cloud STT was used instead)

### Known Limitations

- **Speaker diarization** — Joinly detects when speech starts/stops but doesn't always identify WHO is speaking in multi-party meetings. This is a known open issue in the repo (#175).
- **CAPTCHA handling** — Google sometimes blocks automated browser joins with CAPTCHA. Workaround: use a long-lived session cookie or manually admit the bot (works fine with lobby).
- **Best for 1-on-1** — Works well in one-on-one conversations. Multi-party (3+ people) gets confusing for the turn detection.
- **No built-in memory** — Joinly starts fresh every meeting. No long-term memory of clients. Use Mem0/Supermemory MCP for this.

**Joinly is now the recommended stack for this user — replaces AgentCall entirely.**

| LLM | DeepSeek (via Joinly) | **$0** (free tier) |
| TTS | ElevenLabs (via Joinly) | ~$0.15/hr or free 10K chars |

Previous stack (AgentCall Base $0.35/hr + ElevenLabs $0.15/hr = $0.50/hr) is replaced by Joinly at **$0 base** + API usage only. Saves $0.35/hr and eliminates the commercial dependency.

## Integration Points

### With Meeting Transcription (Vexa + Speaches)

The LiveKit voice agent and Vexa meeting recorder are independent:
- **Vexa** → records and transcribes meetings (post-meeting)
- **AgentCall** → joins meetings and talks in real-time
- **LiveKit** → one-on-one voice chat with AI

They can be combined by pointing the AI agent (via AgentCall) at the same meeting that Vexa is recording — the Vexa recording serves as a backup transcript while AgentCall handles the live conversation.

### With Hermes

The `ask_hermes` function tool in the voice agent allows the vocal AI to delegate tasks to Hermes' backend. This means you can say "Ask Hermes to research this project" and the voice agent forwards the request to Hermes' API endpoint.

### GitHub MCP Server (Repo Access for Agents)

**GitHub's official MCP Server** (29,660⭐) gives agents full read/write access to repos. Deployed as a Docker container that Hermes connects to via `docker exec -i`.

**Deployment:**
```bash
docker run -d --name github-mcp \
  --restart unless-stopped \
  --network agent-net \
  -e GITHUB_TOKEN="ghp_your_token" \
  node:20-slim \
  sh -c "
    mkdir -p /app && cd /app
    npm init -y >/dev/null 2>&1
    npm install @modelcontextprotocol/server-github >/dev/null 2>&1
    while true; do cat /dev/null; sleep 60; done
  "
```

**Container exits immediately with "running on stdio"** — The server-github MCP package expects to read from stdin/write to stdout. It's designed to be invoked per-request by an MCP host (like Hermes). The container just needs to keep alive for the package to be installed — the actual tool calls happen via `docker exec -i` which provides stdin/stdout per invocation.

**Tools available (42+):**
- `create_or_update_file`, `push_files` — write to repos
- `create_repository`, `fork_repository` — manage repos
- `search_repositories`, `search_code`, `get_me` — search and read
- `create_pull_request`, `merge_pull_request` — PR management
- `create_issue`, `list_issues`, `add_issue_comment` — issue tracking

**Wiring into Hermes config.yaml:**
```yaml
mcp_servers:
  github:
    transport: stdio
    command: docker
    args:
      - exec
      - "-i"
      - github-mcp
      - sh
      - "-c"
      - "cd /app && node node_modules/@modelcontextprotocol/server-github/dist/index.js"
```

**Container keeps alive with infinite sleep** — the process in the container does nothing (sleep loop). The `docker exec -i` call spawns a new node process per invocation.

**Pitfall: GITHUB_TOKEN vs GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN** — The server-github package reads `GITHUB_TOKEN`. If you use `GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN`, the env var is ignored and auth fails silently. Set BOTH in the container environment:
```bash
-e GITHUB_TOKEN="ghp_xxx" \
-e GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN="ghp_xxx"
```

**Testing the connection (from host):**
```bash
echo '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list","params":{}}' | timeout 10 docker exec -i github-mcp sh -c "cd /app && node node_modules/@modelcontextprotocol/server-github/dist/index.js" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.loads(sys.stdin.read()); print(f'Tools: {len(d[\"result\"][\"tools\"])}')"
```

### Per-Client Agent System

The `create-agent.py` tool at `/root/ai-agents/` creates dedicated AI agents per client repo. Each agent has:

- **GitHub repo access** (via GitHub MCP server)
- **Client profile** (business info, contact, goals)
- **Structured interview questions** (10 questions covering business, scope, timeline, budget)
- **Meeting memory** (past transcripts and summaries, persistent)
- **Custom system prompt** (agent's role, responsibilities, interview protocol)

**Creating a client agent:**
```bash
python3 /root/ai-agents/create-agent.py create <client-name> <repo-url> '<client-info>'
```

**Example — Nexa Paraguay agent:**
```bash
python3 /root/ai-agents/create-agent.py create "nexa-paraguay" \
  "https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/nexa-paraguay" \
  'Client: Sonia. Business: Helps Dutch expats relocate to Paraguay. Services: residency visas, property, banking. Website: nexa.paragu-ai.com (4 languages).'
```

/root/ai-agents/nexa-paraguay/

# Hermes Agent Full Upgrade & Hardening

Apply all production-grade settings, optional skills, hooks, and optimizations to a Hermes Agent installation (v0.13.0+).

## Prerequisites

- Hermes Agent installed and running (`hermes --version`)
- At least one provider configured (DeepSeek, OpenRouter, etc.)
- Gateway running (`hermes gateway status`)

## Step 1: Audit Current State

```bash
hermes --version
hermes config check
hermes gateway status
hermes profile list
find ~/.hermes/skills -name 'SKILL.md' | wc -l
ls ~/.hermes/hooks/ 2>/dev/null || echo "No hooks"
ls ~/.hermes/plugins/ 2>/dev/null || echo "No plugins"
```

Check which API keys are configured:
```bash
cat ~/.hermes/.env | sed 's/=.*/=***/' | head -40
```

## Step 1b: Detect Local Patches Before Upgrading

Before pulling upstream changes, check for local modifications:

```bash
cd ~/.hermes/hermes-agent
git diff --stat HEAD   # list modified files
```

**If you have local uncommitted changes (patches, customizations):**
1. Stash them: `git stash push -m "update-YYYY-MM-DD"`
2. Pull: `git pull --ff-only origin main`
3. Reinstall deps (use uv, not pip): `uv pip install -e ".[all]" --python venv/bin/python`
4. Apply stash: `git stash pop`
5. Verify: `git diff --stat HEAD` — should show only your original patches

**PITFALL**: If upstream changed files you also modified, `git stash pop` may produce merge conflicts. Resolve manually: `git stash drop` and re-apply your changes by hand.
**PITFALL**: Always check `git diff --stat HEAD` BEFORE and AFTER the stashing cycle. If `git stash pop` failed silently, re-apply with `git stash apply`.

## Step 1c: v0.13.0 Key New Features to Check

v0.13.0 "The Tenacity Release" (May 7, 2026, 864 commits) added major features:

| Feature | How to Enable | Why |
|---------|--------------|-----|
| **Multi-Agent Kanban** (durable) | Already bundled — `hermes kanban boards list` | Durable board with heartbeats, zombie detection, retry budgets, hallucination gate |
| **`/goal` persistent goals** | Add `goals:` section to config.yaml (see Step 2b) | Lock agent onto a target across turns |
| **`video_analyze` tool** | `hermes tools enable video` | Native video understanding via Gemini — no ffmpeg |
| **Post-write delta lint** | Auto-enabled | Lint Python/JSON/YAML/TOML on every write_file/patch |
| **Secret redaction ON by default** | Auto-enabled (`security.redact_secrets: true`) | Prevents credential leaks in tool output |
| **Platform allowlists** | Add `allowed_channels`/`allowed_chats` to platform config | Telegram/Discord/WhatsApp scope control |
| **Session auto-resume** | Set `checkpoints.auto_resume: true` | Sessions survive gateway restarts |
| **google_meet plugin** | `hermes plugins enable google_meet` | Built-in Google Meet join + transcribe + optional speaking |
| **`no_agent` cron mode** | Add `script:` instead of `prompt:` in cron job | Zero-token healthchecks — empty stdout = silent |
| **i18n / display.language** | Set `display.language: es` | Static messages in 7 locales |
| **`hermes curator archive`/`prune`** | CLI subcommands | Archive/prune stale skills |
| **API Server `X-Hermes-Session-Key`** | Auto-enabled | Stable session identifier for dashboard |
| **Curator Run (sync)** | `hermes curator run` | No polling — runs synchronously now |
| **Gateway auto-resume** | Auto-enabled with checkpoints v2 | Sessions recover after gateway crash |

## Step 2: Install All Optional Skills

Optional skills live in `~/.hermes/hermes-agent/optional-skills/`. Copy them into the main skills dir:

```bash
OPT=~/.hermes/hermes-agent/optional-skills
DST=~/.hermes/skills

for cat in security blockchain mcp research creative devops productivity communication health email mlops autonomous-ai-agents; do
  if [ -d "$OPT/$cat" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$DST/$cat"
    cp -rn "$OPT/$cat"/* "$DST/$cat"/ 2>/dev/null
    echo "✓ $cat"
  fi
done
```

Also add external_dirs to config.yaml so skills are discoverable without copying:
```yaml
skills:
  external_dirs:
    - ~/.hermes/hermes-agent/optional-skills
```

## Step 2b: Enable Goals Loop + Kanban Swarms (v0.13.0+)

### Goals Loop — Persistent Ralph-Loop Objectives

```yaml
goals:
  enabled: true
  default_budget: 30                          # turns per goal
  judge_model: google/gemini-2.5-flash        # evaluates completion
  judge_provider: openrouter
  max_continuation_turns: 50
```

**Why:** The goals loop is the single biggest productivity multiplier in v0.13. Instead of prompting the agent every turn, set one goal and let it work. The judge model evaluates completion each turn. This changes Hermes from "chatbot you babysit" to "system you supervise."

**Commands:**
- `/goal do X` — set a persistent goal
- `/goal status` — check progress
- `/goal pause` — pause goal tracking
- `/goal resume` — resume
- `/goal clear` — remove current goal

**Best practice:** Set goals for multi-step tasks. Pair with `delegation.max_spawn_depth: 2` so goals can spawn subagents.

### Kanban Swarms — Multi-Agent Durable Board

Activate the Kanban plugin (bundled in v0.13):

```yaml
plugins:
  enabled:
    - kanban    # add for swarm workflow
```

Create agent profiles for specialized roles:

```yaml
profiles:
  seo-researcher:
    model: deepseek-chat
    provider: deepseek
    toolsets: [web, file, terminal]
  seo-writer:
    model: deepseek-chat
    provider: deepseek
    toolsets: [file, terminal]
  seo-publisher:
    model: deepseek-chat
    provider: deepseek
    toolsets: [terminal, file]
  seo-reviewer:
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash
    provider: openrouter
    toolsets: [file]
```

Add a Kanban board:

```yaml
kanban:
  boards:
    seo-pipeline:
      columns: [keyword-discovery, content-planning, writing, formatting, review, publishing]
      workers:
        - profile: seo-researcher
          column: keyword-discovery
        - profile: seo-writer
          column: writing
        - profile: seo-publisher
          column: publishing
        - profile: seo-reviewer
          column: review
```

**PITFALL:** The Kanban plugin only loads as a dispatcher-spawned worker, not interactively. Verify via `hermes plugin list` and test with `hermes kanban list`.
**PITFALL:** Delegation depth must be >= 2 for multi-agent pipelines. Set `max_spawn_depth: 2` in `delegation` section.

### Curator Tuning

```yaml
curator:
  enabled: true
  interval_hours: 168
  min_idle_hours: 2
  archive_after_days: 90
  stale_after_days: 30
  auxiliary:
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash    # Gemini rates skill quality better than groq
    provider: openrouter
```

Pin critical skills to protect from Curator:
```bash
hermes curator pin "hermes-full-upgrade"
hermes curator pin "elviajero-reference"
hermes curator pin "golden-visa-advisory"
hermes curator pin "git-workflow"
```

## Step 2c: Google Meet Plugin (v0.13.0+)

The built-in `google_meet` plugin (v0.2.0) lets Hermes join Google Meet calls as a participant — no Docker, no Vexa, no AgentCall.

### Prerequisites

```yaml
plugins:
  enabled:
    - google_meet
```

```bash
hermes meet setup          # verify Playwright + Chromium
hermes meet auth           # optional: save Google sign-in (avoids guest lobby)
```

### Two Modes

**Mode 1: Transcribe-only (works immediately, free)**
- Joins meeting, scrapes live captions via DOM MutationObserver
- Zero API keys needed. Zero cost.
```bash
hermes meet join https://meet.google.com/abc-defg-hij
hermes meet transcript
hermes meet stop
```

**Mode 2: Realtime Duplex (bot can speak)**
- Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` in .env (uses OpenAI Realtime API for TTS)
- Depends on PulseAudio null-sink virtual audio device
```bash
hermes meet install --realtime   # installs pulseaudio-utils
hermes meet join https://meet.google.com/abc-defg-hij --mode realtime
hermes meet say "Hello everyone"
```

### Comparison: Plugin vs Custom Stack

| Aspect | Plugin (google_meet) | Custom Stack (Vexa + Speaches + Joinly) |
|--------|---------------------|[REDACTED_TOKEN]|
| **Cost to transcribe** | **$0** (DOM scraping) | $0 (Speaches local) or $0.46/hr (Deepgram) |
| **Cost to speak** | $3.60-6.00/hr (OpenAI Realtime) | $0.15/hr (ElevenLabs) |
| **Platforms** | Google Meet only | Google Meet, Zoom, Teams |
| **Recording** | ❌ Captions only, no audio | ✅ Full audio recording to MinIO |
| **Infra** | Playwright + Chromium (~1,500 LOC) | 12+ containers (Vexa, Speaches, MinIO, Postgres, Redis) |
| **STT quality** | Google Meet captions (English-biased) | Deepgram nova-3 (multilingual, diarization) |
| **TTS quality** | OpenAI Realtime (limited voices) | ElevenLabs (best-in-class) |
| **Dependencies** | OpenAI API key (for speech only) | API keys for Deepgram/ElevenLabs |
| **OpenAI lock-in** | ✅ Yes (for speaking mode) | ❌ No |
| **Remote node** | ✅ Chrome runs on user's Mac via WebSocket | ❌ All server-side |

**Recommendation: Dual strategy** — Use plugin for quick ad-hoc transcription (free, instant). Keep custom stack for calls requiring premium TTS/STT, recordings, or multi-platform support.

## Step 2d: video_analyze Tool (v0.13.0+)

Enable and configure native video understanding:

```bash
hermes tools enable video
```

Set auxiliary model in config.yaml:
```yaml
auxiliary:
  video:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash     # Must support video_url content type
```

Capabilities:
- Formats: mp4, webm, mov, avi, mkv, mpeg
- Size: 50MB hard cap, 20MB warning
- Timeout: 180s floor (videos take longer)
- No ffmpeg needed — native base64 data URL transfer

## Step 2e: no_agent Cron Mode (v0.13.0+)

Script-only watchdog cron jobs — zero LLM token cost. Empty stdout = silent. Non-empty stdout = delivered verbatim.

```yaml
cron:
  jobs:
    - name: disk-health
      script: /root/scripts/disk-check.sh
      schedule: "*/15 * * * *"
      deliver: whatsapp:weissvanderpol
```

**Perfect for:** Healthchecks, site monitors, disk alerts, any deterministic script that produces output only when there's something to report.

## Step 2f: Instant Improvements from v0.13.0

### Session Auto-Resume

Sessions survive gateway restarts:

```yaml
checkpoints:
  enabled: true
  auto_resume: true
  auto_resume_threshold: 50
  max_snapshots: 50
  max_total_size_mb: 500
```

### i18n Display Language

```yaml
display:
  language: es     # 7 locales: zh, ja, de, es, fr, uk, tr
```

### Post-Write Delta Lint (auto-enabled)

Write_file and patch now run in-proc linters for Python, JSON, YAML, TOML. Only new errors introduced by the change are shown — pre-existing errors are hidden.

### Curator Archive/Prune

New CLI subcommands for skill library management:
```bash
hermes curator archive <skill-name>           # Archive without deleting
hermes curator prune                           # Auto-prune stale skills
hermes curator list-archived                   # See archived skills
hermes curator backup                          # Backup before pruning
hermes curator rollback                        # Restore from backup
```

## Step 2g: Platform Allowlists (v0.13.0+ Security)

Scope Hermes to specific channels/chats:

```yaml
telegram:
  allowed_chats:
    - -[REDACTED_PHONE]    # group chat ID
  allowed_users:
    - [REDACTED_PHONE]         # your telegram ID

whatsapp:
  allowed_users:
    - [REDACTED_PHONE]

discord:
  allowed_guilds:
    - "[REDACTED_PHONE]"
  allowed_channels:
    - "[REDACTED_PHONE]"
```

**PITFALL:** Without allowlists, Hermes processes ALL traffic. Set at least one allowlist per platform.

## Step 2h: Security Wave (v0.13.0 — 8 P0 Closures)

v0.13.0 closed 8 P0 security vulnerabilities. All of these are auto-enabled:

| Fix | What Changed |
|-----|-------------|
| **Secret redaction ON by default** | Tool output redacts API keys/secrets in responses |
| **Discord role-allowlists guild-scoped** | Cross-guild DM bypass closed (CVSS 8.1) |
| **WhatsApp rejects strangers** | Never responds in self-chat, denies unknown users |
| **MCP OAuth TOCTOU** | Race condition closed on credential saving |
| **auth.json TOCTOU** | Race condition closed in credential writers |
| **Browser SSRF floor** | Cloud metadata SSRF prevention in hybrid routing |
| **Cron prompt-injection scan** | Scans assembled skill content for injections |
| **`hermes debug share` redaction** | Logs redacted at upload time |

**Verify all are active:**
```bash
grep -c "redact_secrets: true" ~/.hermes/config.yaml
hermes config show | grep -i security
```

## Step 2i: Security Hardening (v0.13.0)

```yaml
security:
  redact_secrets: true          # ON by default in v0.13
  tirith_enabled: true           # gate tool calls
  website_blocklist:
    enabled: true
    domains:
      - malware.com
```

**PITFALL:** `redact_secrets: true` causes IRREVERSIBLE data loss on write — when active, ANY write to .env or config files through Hermes tools replaces API key values with `***` on disk. This is NOT just a display filter — files are permanently corrupted.
- **Workaround:** Use raw Python `open()` in a `terminal` tool call (NOT execute_code), which bypasses Hermes file I/O.
- **BACKUP before any secret write operation:** `cp ~/.hermes/.env ~/.hermes/.env.bak.$(date +%s)`
- **If corrupted:** restore from backup, or re-enter keys manually.

```

### 3g. Delegation Model — CRITICAL: Must be a coding-capable model
```yaml
delegation:
  model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4    # NOT gemini-flash — too weak for coding
  provider: openrouter
  max_concurrent_children: 3
  max_spawn_depth: 2
  orchestrator_enabled: true
  subagent_auto_approve: false
```

**IMPORTANT**: gemini-2.5-flash CANNOT reliably write Python, debug FastAPI, or run pytest loops. Using it for delegation/cron coding tasks wastes tokens on failures and retries. Use claude-sonnet-4.

## Step 4: Create Hooks

### Activity Logger
```bash
mkdir -p ~/.hermes/hooks/activity-logger
```

HOOK.yaml:
```yaml
name: activity-logger
description: Log all agent activity with timestamps for analytics
events:
  - agent:start
  - agent:end
  - agent:step
  - session:start
  - session:end
  - command:*
```

handler.py — logs to ~/.hermes/logs/activity.jsonl

### Long Task Alert
```bash
mkdir -p ~/.hermes/hooks/long-task-alert
```

HOOK.yaml subscribes to `agent:step`, handler logs warning at 15+ iterations.

### PR Watcher (track throughput)
Subscribes to `agent:end`, logs when PRs are created.

### Error Tracker (reliability monitoring)
Subscribes to `agent:step`, logs sessions exceeding 20 iterations (catches loops/failures).

**Validate API keys (common silent failure):**
```python
with open('/root/.hermes/.env') as f:
    for line in f:
        if '=' in line and not line.startswith('#'):
            k, v = line.strip().split('=', 1)
            if 'KEY' in k or 'TOKEN' in k:
                if len(v) < 5:
                    print(f"WARNING: {k} looks empty/broken (len={len(v)})")

import json
auth = json.load(open('/root/.hermes/auth.json'))
for provider, entries in auth.get('credential_pool', {}).items():
    for i, e in enumerate(entries):
        key = e.get('api_key', '')
        if len(key) < 10 or key == 'N/A':
            print(f"WARNING: {provider}[{i}] has empty/placeholder api_key")
```

## Pitfalls

### Config & Credential Management

1. **`redact_secrets: true` causes IRREVERSIBLE data loss on write** — when active, ANY write to .env or config files through Hermes tools replaces API key values with `***` on disk. This is NOT just a display filter — files are permanently corrupted.
   - **Workaround**: Use raw Python `open()` in a `terminal` tool call (NOT execute_code), which bypasses Hermes file I/O.
   - **BACKUP before any secret write**: `cp ~/.hermes/.env ~/.hermes/.env.bak.$(date +%s)`

2. **Systemd user service doesn't source `.env`** — the user-level `hermes-gateway.service` has no `EnvironmentFile=`. Config values using `${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}` syntax are NOT expanded. Inline the actual token in config.yaml.

3. **gateway_state.json caches old config values** — after fixing tokens, update the state file or delete it and restart.

4. **`hermes cron edit` does NOT accept `--model` or `--provider`** — edit `/root/.hermes/cron/jobs.json` directly with Python/sed.

5. **Cron jobs have baked-in model/provider** — each cron job in `jobs.json` stores its own `model`, `provider`, and `base_url`. Config changes alone don't affect them.

6. **Credential pool can have empty api_key entries** — `auth.json`'s `credential_pool` may contain entries with `api_key: ''`. These pass checks but fail with 401 on every call. Clear empty entries.

7. **WhatsApp bridge reads .env, not config.yaml** — `WHATSAPP_ALLOWED_USERS` must be set in `.env` for the Node.js bridge. Set BOTH files for reliable access control.

8. **`npx` MCP servers may print banners** — `npx github-mcp-server` prints a CLI banner that breaks JSON-RPC. Use `--package=github-mcp-server github-mcp-server` with `--silent` flag instead.

9. **WhatsApp bridge exit code -15 causes restart loop** — the Node.js bridge exits with SIGTERM which triggers `Restart=on-failure`. Set `WHATSAPP_ENABLED=false` in .env to isolate.

10. **DeepSeek V3 is the sweet spot for trivial auxiliary tasks** — cheaper than gemini-flash, equally capable for simple extraction/matching/approval.

11. **gemini-2.5-flash can't code** — fails at Python/FastAPI tasks in delegation and cron. Use claude-sonnet-4 for any code generation.

12. **Pin auxiliary models explicitly** — `auto` probes providers on every call. Pinning saves latency and avoids surprise routing.

13. **GitHub MCP: `npx github-mcp-server` prints a CLI banner** — use `@modelcontextprotocol/server-github` instead, or add `--silent` flag.

14. **`@modelcontextprotocol/server-github` IS ALSO DEPRECATED** — Use `--package=github-mcp-server github-mcp-server` with `--silent` flag.

15. **No OpenRouter key? Everything still references it** — check `auxiliary.*.provider`, `delegation.provider`, `smart_model_routing`, `fallback_providers`, ALL cron jobs in `jobs.json`. Batch-fix cron with Python.
", "name": "hermes-full-upgrade"}

Generated: May 11, 2026
Context: Full infrastructure audit and planning session

## Google Meet: Plugin vs Custom Infrastructure

| Dimension | google_meet Plugin (Hermes) | Custom Stack (LiveKit + Vexa + Speaches + AgentCall/Joinly) |
|---|---|---|
| Meeting Platform | Google Meet only | Google Meet, Zoom, Teams |
| Browser Automation | Playwright Chromium (1 Chrome per meeting) | AgentCall handles it server-side, or Joinly handles it |
| STT | Google Meet live captions (DOM scraping) — **$0** | Speaches Whisper (local, CPU) or Deepgram nova-3 ($0.46/hr) |
| TTS | OpenAI Realtime API (must have OPENAI_API_KEY) | ElevenLabs ($0.15/hr), Speaches local, or Piper (free) |
| Can speak without OpenAI? | **No** — realtime mode hard-depends on OpenAI Realtime WS | **Yes** — ElevenLabs, Speaches local, Piper |
| Recording | ❌ No audio recording | ✅ Full audio to MinIO/Postgres |
| Infrastructure | Playwright + Chromium + PulseAudio (~1,500 LOC) | 12+ containers (Vexa, Speaches, MinIO, Postgres, Redis) |
| Cost/hr (transcribe) | $0 | $0 (Speaches local) |
| Cost/hr (speak) | $3.60-6.00 (OpenAI Realtime) | $0.15-0.46 (ElevenLabs + Deepgram) |
| Transcript format | Plain text file `[HH:MM:SS] Speaker: text` | Structured Postgres + MinIO |
| Session durability | One meeting per (gateway, node) pair | Theoretically unlimited |
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Remote node | ✅ Chrome runs on user's Mac via WebSocket RPC | ❌ Everything server-side |

## Decision Matrix

| If you need... | Use... | Because... |
|---|---|---|
| Quick transcript of a Google Meet | `hermes meet join <url>` | Free, instant, no infra |
| Bot speaks in meeting, no OpenAI | Joinly (custom stack) | Plugin requires OpenAI for speech |
| Recording + searchable archive | Vexa stack | Plugin has no recording |
| Multi-platform (Teams, Zoom) | Vexa + Joinly | Plugin is Google Meet only |
| Multilingual STT with diarization | Deepgram + Vexa | Plugin uses English-biased captions |
| Premium TTS (ElevenLabs quality) | Joinly + ElevenLabs | Plugin uses OpenAI Realtime (worse quality, higher cost) |
| $0 operating cost | Plugin (transcribe mode) | Zero API keys needed |

## Joinly vs AgentCall (May 2026)

| | Joinly (open-source) | AgentCall (commercial) |
|---|---|---|
| Cost | **$0** (VPS resources) | $0.35/hr base |
| License | MIT | Proprietary |
| STT | Whisper (local) or Deepgram API | Deepgram under hood |
| TTS | Kokoro (local) or ElevenLabs API | Kokoro at $8/hr markup |
| Meeting join | Google Meet, Zoom, Teams | Same |
| Interruption handling | ✅ Built-in VAD-based turn detection | ✅ Barge-in prevention |
| Multi-party (3+ people) | ❌ Confusing turn detection | ✅ Better |
| Tested in production | ✅ Yes (May 10 2026) | ✅ Yes |

**Recommendation:** Joinly has replaced AgentCall as the recommended stack for this user. It eliminates the $0.35/hr commercial dependency and works with the API keys already configured.
"}
